title: Supply Chain Incident Response Task Tracking

spl:
index=itsm sourcetype=supply_chain_incident earliest=-180d status!="Closed"
| eval due=strptime(remediation_due,"%Y-%m-%d")
| where due < now()
| stats values(task) as open_tasks by incident_id, vendor, component
| sort - incident_id
description: We monitor open tasks for supply chain incidents affecting registered vendor components used in BES Cyber Systems.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes supply_chain_incident, supply_chain_incident`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Create ticket type for supply chain cases. (2) Link tasks to asset inventory rows. (3) Daily stand-up dashboard. (4) Close with compensating control evidence if needed. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=supply_chain_incident`, `sourcetype=supply_chain_incident``.
visualization: Table (overdue incidents), Column chart (by vendor), Single value (open)

grandmaExplanation: We watch for we monitor open tasks for supply chain incidents affecting registered vendor components used in bes cyber systems. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=itsm` `sourcetype=supply_chain_incident`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Create ticket type for supply chain cases. (2) Link tasks to asset inventory rows. (3) Daily stand-up dashboard. (4) Close with compensating control evidence if needed.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=supply_chain_incident earliest=-180d status!="Closed"
| eval due=strptime(remediation_due,"%Y-%m-%d")
| where due < now()
| stats values(task) as open_tasks by incident_id, vendor, component
| sort - incident_id
```

Understanding this SPL

**Supply Chain Incident Response Task Tracking (CIP-013-1 R4 Part 4.1)** — We monitor open tasks for supply chain incidents affecting registered vendor components used in BES Cyber Systems.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=itsm` `sourcetype=supply_chain_incident`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsm; **sourcetype**: supply_chain_incident. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=itsm, sourcetype=supply_chain_incident, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **due** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where due < now()` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by incident_id, vendor, component** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (overdue incidents), Column chart (by vendor), Single value (open)
